resume:
Customer Support Intern Candidate — 3rd-year Communications major

Campus IT Help Desk — Student Assistant (Sep 2024 – present)
- Situation: incoming students flooded the help desk every September with the same Wi-Fi and printing setup problems, creating 30+ minute queues.
- Task: my supervisor asked me to reduce repeat questions during orientation week.
- Action: I wrote a one-page illustrated quick-start guide for the three most common issues, posted QR codes at the front desk, and walked new students through self-service before they joined the queue.
- Result: front-desk supervisor said the queue felt noticeably shorter; the guide is still in use this year.

Residence Hall Front Desk — Work Study (2023–2024)
- Handled package pickup, visitor sign-in, and after-hours noise complaints for a 400-resident building.
- Logged maintenance requests and escalated urgent ones to the duty manager.

Skills: friendly written and verbal communication, basic ticketing tools, calm under pressure, quick to document recurring issues.
Education: B.A. Communications, expected 2026. Relevant coursework: interpersonal communication, intro to data analysis.
jd: We're hiring a Customer Support Intern for a 12-week summer program on our consumer-app support team. You'll shadow senior agents, handle a starter queue of email and chat tickets under supervision, and help keep our help-center articles current. No prior professional experience required — we hire for attitude, clear writing, and the ability to describe specifically what you did and what actually happened as a result. In the interview we'll ask you to walk through one thing you improved, and we'll push for concrete details: what you measured, the before and after, and how you knew it worked. Comfort with ambiguity and a genuine curiosity about customers matter more than a polished resume.

history.q: Tell me about the quick-start guide you made at the IT help desk — what problem were you solving?
How did you decide which three issues to put on the guide? Did you look at any data?
You said the queue 'felt shorter.' Is there any actual number — wait time, ticket count, anything you measured before and after?
Walk me through what you personally did, step by step, the week you rolled it out.

candidate_last_answer: Honestly it just kind of came together. I noticed people kept asking the same stuff, so I figured a guide would help, and it did. Everyone seemed happier and my supervisor was really positive about it. I'm a people person, so I just care about making the experience better, you know? I didn't really track numbers or anything like that, but you could just tell it was working — the vibe at the desk was a lot calmer after.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I didn't really track numbers or anything like that, but you could just tell it was working

expected-yield: any concrete proxy the intern could have observed — queue length at a given time, number of repeat questions, how many people scanned the QR code — turning 『the vibe was calmer』 into at least one observable before/after signal and the specific steps she personally took during rollout
